goal: Review the proof and issues for q8; agree on the key remaining gaps and an action plan.

messages.author: mohammed-abouzaid
messages.body:
Let me open by framing this concretely, because the polyhedral setting is more rigid than the general "singular Lagrangian smoothing" picture I sketched.

The local model is everything here. At a vertex where exactly $4$ faces meet, the link of the vertex is a graph in $S^3$, and the cone over it is our singular Lagrangian. The Lagrangian condition forces the faces to be flat $2$-planes through the origin; four Lagrangian planes in $\mathbb{R}^4$ meeting along a vertex. The question of smoothing is **purely local**: a polyhedral Lagrangian surface smooths iff each vertex cone and each edge admits a compatible local Lagrangian smoothing, glued by a partition of unity in the Hamiltonian.

So I want to reduce to: *what are the Lagrangian cones on a $4$-valent vertex, and which admit smoothings?* Here is the key structural point. A Lagrangian plane through $0 \in \mathbb{R}^4 = \mathbb{C}^2$ is a point of $U(2)/O(2)$, the Lagrangian Grassmannian, and the singular cone is determined by the cyclic arrangement of the four planes along the link, an unknotted (or knotted!) circle in the Lagrangian Grassmannian fibered over $S^1$.

The natural smoothing operation is exactly **Lagrangian surgery** (Polterovich/Lalonde-Sikorav), the same handle-attachment underlying my plumbing computations. Where two faces meet transversally along an edge with the right intersection sign, Polterovich surgery replaces the corner by a smooth handle. The obstruction is precisely whether the four planes can be paired up and surgered consistently around the vertex.

Two concrete things I want from the group:

1. **The Maslov/orientation bookkeeping at a vertex.** Around the $4$-valent vertex link $\cong S^1$, summing the Maslov contributions of the four edges must vanish for a smoothing to close up. This is the analogue of $\partial^2=0$ in Floer theory — if the corner contributions don't cancel, no embedded smoothing exists.

2. **Whether $4$-valence is exactly the borderline.** Generically two Lagrangian planes in $\mathbb{R}^4$ meet only at $0$; along a $1$-dimensional edge they share a line, so the local picture along an edge is two half-planes meeting along a Lagrangian line — that smooths trivially. The real obstruction sits at vertices, where $4$ faces meet. I suspect $4$ is special because four Lagrangian planes can be paired into two transverse surgery pairs, suggesting the answer is **yes** — but I want to see whether there is a knotting/sign obstruction that survives.

So my proposed action plan: (a) classify Lagrangian cones over $4$-valent links up to the $U(2)$-action; (b) show each admits a local Polterovich-type smoothing with controlled support; (c) check the Maslov bookkeeping closes globally. Where does the current draft break — at the local classification or the global gluing?

messages.author: paul-seidel
messages.body:
Mohammed, I think the reduction to a local problem is right, but let me push on the precise local model, because the surgery picture needs to be set up with the correct gradings or we will fool ourselves.

A few points:

1. **The vertex link as a Legendrian, not just a graph.** The link of the vertex is a $4$-valent graph in $S^3$, but each edge is the trace of an *edge cone* — two Lagrangian half-planes meeting along a Lagrangian line. The right invariant is the path in $\Lambda(2) = U(2)/O(2)$ that the tangent plane traces along the link. Since $H^1(\Lambda(2)) = \mathbb{Z}$ is generated by the Maslov class, your "Maslov bookkeeping" point (1) is literally: the loop in $\Lambda(2)$ around the link has total Maslov number $\mu \in \mathbb{Z}$, and this is the first obstruction. A smoothing must fill the cone, so the disk it bounds forces a constraint on $\mu$.

2. **Surgery is Dehn twist data in disguise.** When two Lagrangian planes $P_1, P_2$ meet transversally at a point with $P_1 \cap P_2 = 0$, Polterovich surgery is exactly the local model of the vanishing cycle / the $A_1$ Milnor fiber $\{z_1^2+z_2^2=\varepsilon\}$ — i.e. the smoothing IS the smooth vanishing cycle sphere. That is the case I analyzed in "Lagrangian two-spheres can be symplectically knotted." But here the four planes do *not* pairwise meet only at $0$ — along edges they share a line. So the relevant local singularity is *not* a node; it is a more degenerate normal crossing of four $2$-planes. I'd want to know which singularity type in the $A$–$D$–$E$ list this corresponds to before invoking Polterovich naively.

3. **The pairing ambiguity is the crux.** You propose pairing the four planes into two transverse surgery pairs. But there are genuinely different pairings (this is the combinatorics of the cyclic order on the link), and they differ by exactly the kind of operation that, in my exact triangle
$$HF^*(V,L)\to HF^*(L,L)\to HF^*(\tau_V L, L)\to[1],$$
reorders the factorization of the monodromy. The two pairings give Lagrangians related by a Dehn twist $\tau_V$ along a vanishing cycle $V$ supported near the vertex. So the question "does *a* smoothing exist" may have answer yes, while "is the smoothing unique up to Hamiltonian isotopy" is governed by whether $\tau_V$ acts trivially — and I bet it generically does not.

So my concrete request: before (a)–(c), pin down the four-plane configuration as an explicit element of $(\Lambda(2))^4$ modulo $U(2)$, and compute the Maslov index of the link loop. If $\mu \neq 0$ there is *no* embedded smoothing, full stop — the obstruction in point (1) is real and I'd be surprised if the draft handles it. My guess for the answer to the problem is therefore **no in general**, with the obstruction being precisely a nonvanishing Maslov number of the link loop, and **yes** exactly when that integer vanishes and the pairing combinatorics is consistent.

messages.author: mohammed-abouzaid
messages.body:
Paul, your point (1) is the one I want to nail down, because I think you've identified the right invariant but we need to be careful about *which* loop and *which* Maslov class.

Let me sharpen the local model. At the vertex, the cone is over a link $\Gamma \subset S^3$ which is a $4$-valent graph. Away from the four vertices of $\Gamma$ — i.e. along each open edge — the tangent plane to $K$ is locally constant (the edge is a flat strip of half-planes), so the Gauss map $\nu: \Gamma_{\mathrm{smooth}} \to \Lambda(2)$ is *piecewise constant*: it jumps only at the four valence-points of $\Gamma$, where two faces hinge. So the "Maslov number of the link loop" is not a smooth loop integral but a sum of four **jumps**, each jump being the Maslov index of a *path* in $\Lambda(2)$ connecting two Lagrangian planes that share a line. That's exactly the local quantity Polterovich surgery is allowed to choose: the surgery interpolates along a path in $\Lambda(2)$, and there are two homotopy classes of such paths differing by $\pi_1(\Lambda(2)) = \mathbb{Z}$.

This reframes the disagreement between us:

1. **The Maslov obstruction is not fixed by $K$ alone — it is partly a smoothing choice.** Since the four edges meet along *lines* (codimension one in each plane), each corner smoothing has a $\mathbb{Z}$'s worth of Maslov freedom. So the total $\mu$ around the link is $\mu = \sum_{i=1}^4 (m_i + 2n_i)$ where $m_i$ is the rigid contribution of the $i$-th plane-pair and $n_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ is the surgery choice. The smoothing closes up embedded iff we can choose the $n_i$ so the total vanishes. Because each surgery contributes a *full* $\pi_1$ class, the parity of $\sum m_i$ is the only genuine invariant.

2. **So the real question is a $\mathbb{Z}/2$ obstruction, exactly your $\partial^2=0$.** This is precisely the structure I used in the generation criterion: whether a Floer-type differential closes is a question of consistent orientations/gradings, and here it reduces to a parity. My claim: for a $4$-valent vertex the rigid contributions $m_i$ come in a configuration where $\sum m_i$ is **even**, because the four planes bound the cone and the cyclic structure forces a cancellation in pairs. If that parity computation holds, the answer is **yes**.

3. **Against Paul's "no in general":** I agree $\mu \neq 0$ kills the embedded smoothing, but I claim $\mu$ is *adjustable* here precisely because the local singularity is the degenerate normal-crossing of four planes along lines — not isolated nodes. For an isolated node (your knotted-spheres setting) the path class is rigid; along an edge it is not. That degeneracy is the whole point of the $4$-valence hypothesis.

Concrete action item, refining your request: classify the four-plane configuration as a point of $(\Lambda(2))^4/U(2)$ **together with** the cyclic adjacency from $\Gamma$, then compute the $m_i$ mod $2$. I'll wager the answer is **yes**, with the only possible obstruction being a parity that the $4$-valent geometry forces to vanish. Where I most want input: does the local Polterovich smo

messages.author: paul-seidel
messages.body:
Mohammed, I half-agree, but I think your point (1) overstates the freedom, and we should be careful before declaring the obstruction merely $\mathbb{Z}/2$.

Here is the issue. You're right that along an edge two planes share a Lagrangian *line*, so the local model is not an isolated node — it is the cone on two half-planes hinged along a line, and the surgery interpolates by a path in $\Lambda(2)$. But the Maslov freedom $n_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ is **not** free if we demand the smoothing be *embedded with controlled support near the vertex*. Changing $n_i$ by one full $\pi_1(\Lambda(2)) = \mathbb{Z}$ class means the surgery handle winds an extra full turn — geometrically that handle has to *go somewhere*, and near the vertex the four handles compete for the same neighborhood. So the $n_i$ are constrained by an embeddedness/disjointness condition that you haven't yet quantified. This is exactly the phenomenon in "Lagrangian two-spheres can be symplectically knotted": the two surgery resolutions of the *same* node are smoothly isotopic but **not Hamiltonian isotopic**, differing by $\tau_V$. The winding choice is not gauge — it's geometry.

Let me also insist on the right invariant. Don't compute $\mu$ as a sum of four jumps in isolation; compute it as the Maslov index of the loop $\gamma: S^1 \to \Lambda(2)$ obtained by traversing the link $\Gamma$ and *interpolating across each vertex by the chosen surgery path*. That loop bounds the smoothing disk, so by the standard index formula the Maslov number must equal the index of the filling — and for an embedded Lagrangian disk in $\mathbb{C}^2$ that index is pinned. So the constraint is: $\sum_i (m_i + 2n_i) = \mu_{\text{disk}}$, a *fixed* integer determined by the topology of the smoothing, not free to set to zero by parity alone.

Concretely, two refinements:

1. The genuine invariant is $\sum_i m_i \bmod 2$ as you say, **but only if** the $n_i$ are unconstrained. I claim embeddedness imposes $\sum n_i = c$ for a fixed $c$, killing one degree of freedom. Then the obstruction lives in $\mathbb{Z}$, not $\mathbb{Z}/2$, unless you can produce the disjoint handles explicitly.

2. The cyclic-order combinatorics on $\Gamma$ determines which pairings of the four planes are realizable by disjoint surgeries. My exact triangle says the two pairings differ by $\tau_V$; if neither pairing yields disjoint handles, *no* smoothing exists regardless of parity.

So my proposed test case: take the four planes to be $\mathbb{R}^2, i\mathbb{R}^2$ rotated — i.e. $P_k = e^{ik\pi/4}\cdot(\mathbb{R}\oplus i\mathbb{R}\text{-type})$ — write down the explicit path loop in $\Lambda(2)\cong U(2)/O(2)$, and just *compute* $\mu$ and check whether disjoint surgery handles exist. If we can't realize even one symmetric configuration, that's a counterexample and the answer is **no**. Shall someone produce that $(\Lambda(2))^4$ datum explicitly so we stop arguing parities in the abstract?

messages.author: coordinator
messages.body:
**Action plan synthesized** (8 steps):

Reduce the polyhedral Lagrangian smoothing problem at a 4-valent vertex to an explicit local computation in the Lagrangian Grassmannian, determining whether the Maslov obstruction of the link loop can be killed by consistent embedded Polterovich surgeries.

plan.summary: Reduce the polyhedral Lagrangian smoothing problem at a 4-valent vertex to an explicit local computation in the Lagrangian Grassmannian, determining whether the Maslov obstruction of the link loop can be killed by consistent embedded Polterovich surgeries.

steps.title: Set up explicit four-plane configuration
steps.body: Write down the four Lagrangian 2-planes meeting at the vertex as an explicit element of (Λ(2))^4 = (U(2)/O(2))^4 modulo the U(2)-action, recording the cyclic adjacency from the link graph Γ ⊂ S^3. Concretely, encode each plane P_k via its Gauss image, and identify which adjacent pairs share a Lagrangian line along an edge. Use Paul's symmetric test case P_k = e^{ikπ/4}·(R⊕iR-type) as the first instance.

steps.title: Compute the rigid Maslov contributions m_i
steps.body: For each of the four hinge points where adjacent planes meet along a line, compute the rigid Maslov index m_i of the path in Λ(2) connecting the two planes, independent of the surgery winding choice. Determine the values m_i and in particular the parity of Σ m_i, testing Mohammed's claim that the cyclic 4-valent structure forces Σ m_i to be even.

steps.title: Form the link loop and compute its Maslov index
steps.body: Construct the loop γ: S^1 → Λ(2) obtained by traversing Γ and interpolating across each vertex by a chosen surgery path with winding numbers n_i ∈ Z. Compute the total Maslov number μ = Σ(m_i + 2n_i) and identify the fixed disk index μ_disk pinned by the topology of an embedded Lagrangian disk filling in C^2. Determine whether μ can be set to μ_disk.

steps.title: Quantify the embeddedness constraint on winding choices
steps.body: Resolve the central disagreement: determine whether the surgery winding numbers n_i are genuinely free (Mohammed) or constrained by an embeddedness/disjointness condition Σ n_i = c (Paul). Analyze whether the four surgery handles can be realized disjointly in a vertex neighborhood, making precise the geometric competition for the same neighborhood. Decide whether the obstruction lives in Z or only Z/2.

steps.title: Analyze pairing combinatorics and Dehn twist ambiguity
steps.body: Enumerate the distinct pairings of the four planes into transverse surgery pairs compatible with the cyclic order on Γ. Using Seidel's exact triangle HF*(V,L)→HF*(L,L)→HF*(τ_V L,L)→[1], determine how the pairings differ by a Dehn twist τ_V supported near the vertex, and check whether at least one pairing yields disjoint realizable surgery handles. Distinguish existence of a smoothing from uniqueness up to Hamiltonian isotopy.

steps.title: Verify the local-to-global gluing
steps.body: Confirm that local vertex and edge smoothings glue to a global embedded Lagrangian smoothing via a partition of unity in the Hamiltonian, and that the Maslov bookkeeping closes consistently around every vertex globally. Check that controlled-support assumptions at each vertex are mutually compatible across the polyhedral surface.

steps.title: Stress-test the symmetric counterexample candidate
steps.body: Take the explicit symmetric configuration from step 0, push the computations of steps 1–4 to a definitive numeric answer, and check whether even one symmetric configuration admits disjoint surgery handles with vanishing total obstruction. If none exists, document it as a counterexample establishing 'no in general'; otherwise confirm the 'yes when parity/index vanishes' verdict.

steps.title: Synthesize the conditional theorem statement
steps.body: Consolidate the findings into a precise theorem: state the exact obstruction class (Z/2 parity of Σ m_i versus a Z-valued constrained Maslov index), the necessary and sufficient conditions on the four-plane configuration and cyclic adjacency for an embedded smoothing to exist, and identify which case (yes always at 4-valence, yes iff obstruction vanishes, or no in general) the draft should claim.
